g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive lesson on the Turing Machine - the foundation of computer science theory.
A Turing Machine is a theoretical computing device with an infinite tape, read-write head, and finite control that simulates any algorithmic computation.
g.text: A theoretical computing device consisting of:
An infinite tape divided into cells
A read-write head that moves left/right

g.data-narration: Alan Turing introduced this concept in 1936 to solve the Entscheidungsproblem and formally define what it means for a problem to be computable.
g.text: 1936: Alan Turing's paper "On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem"
Transformed the vague concept of "algorithm" into a rigorous mathematical definition
Foundation for all modern computation theory

g.data-narration: A Turing Machine is formally defined as a 7-tuple M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject) representing states, alphabets, transitions, and special states.
g.text: M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject)

g.data-narration: The tape is the memory of the Turing Machine - infinite in length, divided into cells, each holding one symbol from the tape alphabet Γ.
g.text: Σ ⊂ Γ (input alphabet is subset of tape alphabet) • Blank symbol (□) marks end of input

g.data-narration: The read-write head reads symbols, writes new ones, and moves left or right. The finite control holds the current state and decides actions.
The transition function δ: Q × Γ → Q × Γ × {L, R} defines the machine's behavior: given a state and symbol, it specifies new state, write symbol, and direction.
g.text: δ: Q × Γ → Q × Γ × {L, R}
"In state q reading X: write Y, move D, go to state p"

g.data-narration: The TM starts in initial state q₀ at the leftmost input cell. For each step, it reads the current symbol, applies transition, and moves the head.
g.text: Start at q₀, head at leftmost cell
If reaches q_accept → ACCEPT | If reaches q_reject → REJECT | If no transition → HALT & REJECT

g.data-narration: The Church-Turing Thesis states that any function computable by any algorithmic process can be computed by a Turing Machine - a foundational principle.
A Deterministic TM has exactly one transition per state-symbol pair. A Non-Deterministic TM allows multiple. Remarkably, both have equal computational power.
g.text: Exactly ONE transition for each (state, symbol)

g.data-narration: Multi-tape TMs use multiple tapes, each with its own head. Multi-head TMs have multiple heads on one tape. Both are computationally equivalent to standard TM.
g.text: All variants are computationally equivalent to the standard single-tape TM

g.data-narration: The Universal Turing Machine can simulate any other Turing Machine. It takes both the input and the description of another TM as its input - the foundation of stored-program computers.
g.text: Output: Same as the simulated TM would produce
Concept predated physical computers by years • Foundation of stored-program computers

g.data-narration: Example: Design a TM for language L = {0ⁿ1ⁿ | n ≥ 1}. The TM marks first 0 and first 1, then checks if all pairs are matched - accepts if equal, rejects otherwise.
g.text: Q = {q₀, q₁, q₂, q_accept} • Σ = {0, 1} • Γ = {0, 1, X, □}
Working: Mark first 0→X, find first 1→X, repeat until all matched, accept at blank

g.data-narration: A language is decidable if a TM halts on every input and correctly accepts or rejects. The Halting Problem is undecidable - no TM can solve it for all inputs.
g.text: ∃ TM that halts on EVERY input and accepts/rejects correctly
NO TM exists that can decide membership for ALL inputs
Halting Problem: The Classic Undecidable Problem

g.data-narration: Remember these key formulas: Transition function δ: Q × Γ → Q × Γ × {L, R}, DTM ≡ NTM, and all TM variants have equal computational power.
Key takeaways: TM is the most powerful computational model, all variants are equivalent, and some problems like the Halting Problem are provably unsolvable.
g.text: ✓ Memorize the 7-tuple: M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject)
✓ Know the difference: Decidable (halts always) vs Undecidable (Halting Problem)
✓ Remember: All variants (multi-tape, multi-head, NTM) = standard TM in power
